coredata.dc:title: Detection of tannic acid exploiting carbon dots enhanced hydrogen peroxide/potassium ferricyanide chemiluminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yue
Yang, Yaqiong
Jiang, Yamei
Han, Suqin
coredata.dc:description: Recently, ultra-weak chemiluminescence (CL) systems have become one of the focuses of increasing attention. As one of them, H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system had also been studied. However, no attempts had been made to investigate the analytical applications of this system based on carbon dots (CDs). In this paper, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) were synthesized using formaldehyde as a coreactant, and it acted as a new fluorophore instead of conventional fluorescent dyes to enhance the CL signal of H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system, in which the excited-state FNCDs was the final emitting species, and was probably produced by the electron transfer and energy from reactive oxygen species. Interestingly, tannic acid (TA) could dramatically suppress the CL intensity of FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system. Under the optimum conditions, a linear relationship existed between reduced CL intensity and logarithm of TA concentration in range between 0.2 and 10.0 μM with a detection limit of 39.3 nM. The method was applied to the quantitative analysis of TA concentration in red wine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Carbon dots
Hydrogen peroxide
Potassium ferricyanide
Tannic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Response of the system to TA detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 2013011013–3 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the FI-CL system. P1 and P2, peristaltic pump; V, valve; HV, negative high voltage; PMT, photomultipier tube.
(a) FL spectra of FNCDs at different temperatures and times, (b) Wide scan XPS spectrum of FNCDs, high-resolution XPS spectra of the (c) C1s, (d) O1s and (e) N1s in FNCDs, (f) TEM image of FNCDs.

(a) The influence of reagent injection orders for CL intensity, inset: CL intensity of H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system, (b) CL spectrum of FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6, (c) the effect of radical scavengers on the CL intensity.
(a) CL intensity of FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system with increasing concentrations of TA, (b) corresponding calibration for the determination of TA.
Tolerance limit of interferences on the determination of TA.
Determination of TA in red wine samples.
Comparison of the results between CL and UV methods.

head.ce:title: Detection of tannic acid exploiting carbon dots enhanced hydrogen peroxide/potassium ferricyanide chemiluminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041000, Shanxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041000, Shanxi, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, ultra-weak chemiluminescence (CL) systems have become one of the focuses of increasing attention. As one of them, H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system had also been studied. However, no attempts had been made to investigate the analytical applications of this system based on carbon dots (CDs). In this paper, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) were synthesized using formaldehyde as a coreactant, and it acted as a new fluorophore instead of conventional fluorescent dyes to enhance the CL signal of H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system, in which the excited-state FNCDs was the final emitting species, and was probably produced by the electron transfer and energy from reactive oxygen species. Interestingly, tannic acid (TA) could dramatically suppress the CL intensity of FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system. Under the optimum conditions, a linear relationship existed between reduced CL intensity and logarithm of TA concentration in range between 0.2 and 10.0 μM with a detection limit of 39.3 nM. The method was applied to the quantitative analysis of TA concentration in red wine.

ce:section.ce:para: The onym “carbon dots (CDs)” refers to zero-dimensional nanomaterials displaying fully-color-tunable fluorescence (FL) emission from the blue to the near-infrared region, which presents a diameter of less than 10 nm and possess excellent water solubility, high chemical stability, low toxicity or eco-friendly, tunable excitation and emission spectra, abundance of raw materials and low cost [1,2]. It is worth mentioning that the main quality of CDs depends on their quantum yield (QY), which is used to evaluate whether CDs are preferable. Later, CDs are further developed and applied on the basis of their subsequent functionalization or element doping, which endow them with high FLQY and high catalytic activity. It is well-known that nitrogen doping can not only improve fluorescence properties of CDs but also allow to provide available functional groups for target sensing [3]. For example, Zhu et al. have verified that the FLQY of prepared CDs was less than 10% when the reactants only contain hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, nevertheless, the presence of one or more amino groups in the reactants could effectively increase the FLQY of most prepared CDs to more than 10% [4]. Whereupon many researchers use different substances containing amino groups as raw materials to prepare CDs, such as ammonia [5,6], amino acid [7–9], ethylenediamine [10,11], ethylenediamine tetraaceticacid [12,13], and so on. Melamine is a cyclic trimer of cyanamide with a 1,3,5-triazine skeleton on which three amino groups has high propensity to react with carboxyl, hydroxyl and ketone [14], and was considered to be suitable molecular precursor for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs due to its richness of nitrogen contents [14–16]. Formaldehyde is of special note not only because it can increase the solubility of melamine but also serves as the cooperative passivating agent to possibly regulate the chemical state of nitrogen in CDs owing to a reaction take place between formaldehyde and melamine [17]. In our work, citric acid (CA), melamine and formaldehyde were used to synthesize FNCDs, and its FLQY was up to 63.7%, which was higher than that of the CDs (31%) synthesized by CA and melamine, demonstrating the key role of formaldehyde in enhancing QY of FNCDs.

To date, CDs-based chemiluminescence (CL) systems have displayed superior CL properties and have been used as analytical tools. Meanwhile, the research on enhancing the inherent sensitivity of CDs to weak CL emission and the expansion of its application range have also gradually aroused research interest. As an significant reagent, hydrogen peroxide induced ultra-weak CL systems have become the most extensively investigated one [18]. For instance, Lin et al. studied the CL property of CDs in the presence of peroxynitrous acid formed by online mixing of nitrite and acidified H2O2 [19]. The effects of carbon nanodots on the ultraweak CL reaction of H2O2 and NaHSO3 were explored [20]. CDs exhibited intense CL enhancement of the H2O2-NaIO4 system [21], and a high emission intensity CL system was established by introducing N-CDs into Fenton system [9]. As one of the ultra-weak CL systems, the H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6CL reaction had also been studied previously, and luminol [22,23] and fluorescein [24] were utilized as sensitizers to increase the CL intensity of this system. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, there is almost no report about the application of CDs to H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system. Compared with conventional reagents, CDs is highly valued for CL usages. Hence there is still a great demand for the development of H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system involving CDs. This undoubtedly provides a new research direction and enlarges the application of the system in many fields. Research showed that the weak CL intensity of H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system could be enhanced by FNCDs.

Tannic acid (TA), a natural polyphenolic compound with one glucose unit and 10 galloyl groups, is a abundant and sustainable natural resource widespread in many plants, vegetables, and fruits [25]. It is usually used as an additive in pharmaceutical industry, mordant in printing and dyeing industry, and flavoring agent in food products [26]. Besides, it has some physiological and pharmacological activities, such as antiinflammatory, antioxidation, antitoxicity and prevention of cardiovascular disease, which endows it with certain medical value. TA in red wine mainly come from the peel and seed of grape. The bitter taste in red wine comes from the role of TA. TA is used as clarifying agent in the brewing and wine industry, and its content is also an essential standard to evaluate their quality. However, TA can cause environmental water contamination due to the application in the tannery industry, and excessive intake of TA can cause abdominal pain, diarrhea, vomiting, circulatory disorders, and even death [27]. Hence, a simple and accurate detection of TA in relevant samples is always been of great importance. So some methods have been developed for the determination of TA, including spectrophotometry [28], colorimetry [29], CL [30], FL [31,32], electrochemistry [33], and so on. In our work, the extensive tests revealed that TA could reduce the intensity of light emitted from the FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6CL reaction. Based on this research, an efficient CL method for the measurement of TA in red wine samples was reported.

All of the chemicals with a suitable analytical grade were applied in the experiments. Deionized water (DI) obtained from a Milli-Q purification system (Millipore) was used to prepare all aqueous solutions. CA, melamine and H2O2 were supplied from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Formaldehyde, K3Fe(CN)6 and TA were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). The stock solution of 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6 was prepared by dissolving appropriate quantity solid in DI. A 1.0 mM stock solution of TA was prepared by dissolving accurately weighed TA solid with DI and diluting to 50 mL. The H2O2 solution was prepared when it will be used.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was recorded with a JEOL JEM-2100 TEM (Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on a Ultima IV-185 XRD (Rigaku, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were taken by a K-Alpha surface analysis system (Thermo, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy analysis was surveyed with a VARIAN 660-IR spectrometer (Varian, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was scanned using a TU-1810 PC spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) in the range of 200–800 nm. The FL emission spectra were performed with a F-380 FL spectrophotometer (Tianjin Gangdong Science and Technology Development Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China). All the CL signals were collected by a model IFIS-C intelligent flow injection (FI) sampler (Xi’an Remax Analytical Instrument Co. Ltd., Shannxi, China) and a BPCL ultra-weak luminescence analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China).

A simple two channel manifold used for TA determination was shown in Fig. 1 . The solutions of water carrier, TA standard/sample and alkaline K3Fe(CN)6 were transported by peristaltic pump (P2) with flow velocity of 2.5 mL/min, the H2O2 and FNCDs solutions were delivered by pump (P1) with flow rate of 1.2 mL/min. The concentration of TA was quantified by measuring the change of CL intensity, ΔI = I − I 0, where I and I 0 were CL signals in the absence and presence of TA, respectively.

The FNCDs herein reported were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal process (Scheme S1). Nevertheless, a suitable reaction condition was essential to the FL properties of the functionalized CDs. To this end, we prepared FNCDs under different reaction conditions, the relationship of the FL intensity with the reaction temperatures and times was presented in Fig. 2 a. It was found that the FL intensity of FNCDs enhanced with the increase of temperature and time to a certain extent. Then the FL intensity reached a maximum value at reaction condition of 180 °C and 12 h. The FNCDs synthesized under this condition was expressed as FNCDs (180–12). Meanwhile, the QY of these FNCDs at different reaction temperatures and times were also investigated. The results showed that the highest QY was obtained by FNCDs (180–12), which was determined to be 63.7% as measured using quinine sulfate as a reference substance. Hence, the FNCDs (180–12) had been selected as the object of further study. Fig. S1a gave the UV–vis absorption spectrum of FNCDs, there was a wide absorption band in the range of 200 nm to 400 nm, attributing to the n–π* transition of CO. Besides, a detailed FL study of FNCDs was carried out at different excitation wavelengths, showing the maximum emission FL at 425 nm with the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Clearly, a slight excitation-dependent FL behavior was observed, shifting to longer wavelengths without a significant change in shape. As in the case of most luminescent CDs, this excitation-dependent FL behavior of the FNCDs reflected the effect of particles of different sizes and a distribution of different surface states. The surface state was analogous to a molecular state whereas the size effect was the result of quantum dimensions, both of which contributed to the complexity of the excited states of FNCDs [3,34].

The element composition and functional groups of prepared FNCDs were testified by XPS and FTIR spectra. As list in Fig. 2b, apart from C1s peak (289.08 eV) and O1s (535.08 eV) peak involved in the full range XPS of the FNCDs, the appearance of N1s located at 400.08 eV well indicated the presence of N atoms in the structure of FNCDs, along with an atomic percentage of 3.83%. The contents of the C and O elements were 69.47% and 26.69%, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) was fine-scanned and deconvoluted into four peaks centered at 284.52 eV, 285.17 eV, 286.31 eV, 288.76 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.68 eV and 532.88 eV, which could be assigned to CO and COH/COC, respectively. Additionally, Fig. 2e demonstrated further the presence of N groups, the peak at 399.61 eV was assigned to NC bonding and the peak at 401.07 eV was attributed to NH bonding. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1b) revealed the presence of characteristic functional groups in the FNCDs, it was possible to detect the stretching vibration bands of OH, NH and CH groups in the range of 2900–3500 cm−1. The peak at 1700 cm−1 could be assigned to the CO stretch vibration. And the CN and COC bonds created clear peaks at 1429 cm−1 and 1174 cm−1, respectively. These peaks indicated that the prepared FNCDs were surrounded by –COOH, OH and NH2 groups. These results provided clear evidence that the nitrogen atoms was successfully adulterated.

The appearance and size of the prepared FNCDs were observed by TEM image (Fig. 2f). The FNCDs were spherical shape, the obtained deep yellow FNCDs exhibited good dispersion in aqueous solution and its average particle size was centered at around 3.7 nm. The XRD profile (Fig. S1c) displayed the diffraction peak at around 22° agreed well with (0 0 2) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials.

The influence of FNCDs on the H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6CL reaction was investigated by static injection mode. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the effect of various injection orders of reagents for CL intensity was studied and showed that the highest CL signal was obtained by injecting K3Fe(CN)6 into the mixture of FNCDs and H2O2 because no reaction occurred between H2O2 and FNCDs before K3Fe(CN)6 was injected. Therefore, the flow path of the final injection of K3Fe(CN)6 was selected, seeing Fig. 1. Additionally, the CL intensity increased about 2000 times after adding FNCDs into H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 solution. Furthermore, the CL intensity of H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system by FNCDs with different synthesis temperatures and times were studied. The results showed that the maximum CL intensity caused by FNCDs prepared at 180–12, which was fit with the FNCDs owning the highest QY mentioned above.

The CL spectrum of FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system was measured using high-energy cutoff filters. As shown in Fig. 3b, the maximum CL spectrum were located in 450–620 nm range with centered at 540 nm. The wide range of CL spectrum was similar to the FL spectrum of FNCDs, and the CL peak position was red-shifted in comparison to the most intense FL peak. The red-shift likely resulted from the smaller energy separations of the FNCDs surface state than that of the energy for the most intense FL [19,35]. It is generally known that CL can be attributed to the various surface energy traps that existed on the FNCDs and FL mainly occurs through excitation and emission within the core of the FNCDs [35,36]. This confirmed that the emitting species in FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system was excited-state FNCDs (FNCDs*).

To study whether free radicals exist in the FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system, the influence of nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT), sodium azide (NaN3) and thiourea, as well-known radical scavengers for
O2 −, 1O2 and
OH [37,38], on the CL intensity of the system was studied, respectively. As can be seen in (Fig. 3c), all these radical scavengers (in 1.0 mM) indicated a notable negative effect on the CL intensity. So
O2 −, 1O2 and
OH produced in the CL reaction process.

Based on the results of these studies, the possible reaction mechanism of the FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system was illustrated in Scheme S2. The
O2 −, formed by the reaction of K3Fe(CN)6 with H2O2 [24], was unstable in aqueous solution initiating the reactive oxygen chain reaction, which led to the generation of the reactive oxygen radical and singlet-oxygen (1O2) [39]. And
OH could be generated through the interaction of
O2 − and H2O2 [40]. The recombination of 1O2 could evolve (1O2)2* [41], which was unstable and converted into O2 by releasing energy to generate a weak CL. The electron donation from
O2 − to FNCDs produced negative charge FNCDs (FNCDs −), K3Fe(CN)6 could inject holes into the FNCDs to produce positive charge FNCDs (FNCDs +), and the
OH also could easily reacted with the FNCDs to form FNCDs +. The FNCDs + and FNCDs − through electron-hole annihilation resulted in energy release in the form of CL emission. On the other hand, the emission wavelength of (1O2)2* could compass to 381 nm which was overlaped the excitation wavelength of FNCDs [42], and the direct energy transfer from (1O2)2* to the FNCDs may also be possible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of the system to TA detection
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the selectivity of the FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system, the effect of some metal ions and organics to this system was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, the CL intensity decreased significantly with the addition of TA, very small changes were observed in the case of resveratrol, hydroquinone and catechol, the effect of other species on intensity could be negligible. This proved that the FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system had a high selectivity for TA detection. TA has abundant hydroxyl groups, which render it easy to interact with some oxidants. The UV–vis spectra of TA, K3Fe(CN)6 and TA-K3Fe(CN)6 was investigated. As listed in Fig. S3, the absorption peaks of TA at 278 nm, K3Fe(CN)6 at 420 nm, 302 nm and 260 nm disappeared in the mixed solution of K3Fe(CN)6 and TA, which meant the redox reaction between K3Fe(CN)6 and TA occurred and consumed part of K3Fe(CN)6. Moreover, TA maybe reacted with
O2 − and
OH in FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system and resulted in a decrease of some relevant species, inhibiting the CL intensity.

The effect of the concentration of H2O2, K3Fe(CN)6, FNCDs and NaOH was studied, and the results showed that the favorable conditions were 0.4 M H2O2, 0.3 mM K3Fe(CN)6 in 0.3 M NaOH and 1:5 (v/v) FNCDs (Fig. S4). Based on this, a CL method to detect TA was developed and the response of the system to different concentrations of TA was investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 4 a. An excellent linear response was achieved between the inhibited CL intensity and log of TA concentration in the range of 0.2 µM to 10.0 μM (Fig. 4b). The linear regression equation can be expressed as ΔI = 4416 + 5915 lg[TA] and correlation coefficient (R) of 0.9971. The detection limit (LOD) was 39.3 nM calculated according to the IUPAC definition 3 s/k , where s was the standard deviation obtained from a series of blank solutions, k was the slope of the calibration graph. The intra-day precision represented by relative standard deviation (RSD) was 1.5% in eleven repeated measurements of 4.0 µM TA. The inter-day precision was calculated to be 2.4% in the replicated measurements of 4.0 µM TA at five different days. This method was applied to determine TA in red wine sample, which was diluted 50 times directly without any treatment. The recovery of 98.9–102.1% was obtained (Table 1 ), demonstrating its reliability and application potential in real sample analysis. The effect of some possible existent species for the determination of TA was evaluated. The tolerable concentration ratios for interferences in relative error of 5% were given in Fig. 5 . The obtained results showed that the method had a good selectivity for the determination of TA in red wine sample. Determination results of TA by the present method were compared with the UV spectrophotometric method. The absorbance of TA was measured at 278 nm. The analytical results for three red wine samples were presented in Table 2 . The t-test showed that there was no significant difference between the two methods.

In summary, FNCDs could sensitize CL intensity of H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system, which was beneficial for its application in CL detection. A new analytical method for the determination of TA was developed based on its inhibition to FNCDs-H2O2-K3Fe(CN)6 system CL emission. The proposed method exhibited high sensitivity and good selectivity, and was applied for the determination of TA in red wine sample. This work presented here may be extended to develop other new FNCDs-based CL systems, which would found extensive application in various fields.

Yue Li, Yaqiong Yang and Yamei Jiang performed the design of experiments, optimization experimental parameters, method validation. Yue Li carried out data analysis and manuscript preparation. Suqin Han performed the conception of study, design of experiments, and manuscript preparation and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 2013011013–3).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105113.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.L. Liu, B.B. Chen, C.M. Li, C.Z. Huang, Carbon dots: synthesis, formation mechanism, fluorescence origin and sensing applications, Green Chem. 21 (2019) 449–471, https://doi.org/10.1039/c8gc02736f.

Rogach, Carbonization conditions influence the emission characteristics and thestability against photobleaching of nitrogen doped carbon dots, Nanoscale 9 (2017) 11730–11738, https://doi.org/10.1039/c7nr03648e.

B 158 (2016) 235–242, http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2016.03.010.

Lumin. 196 (2018) 100–110, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2017.12.029.
